scenario: A team has a base Connector Element Template for "Generic REST Call" with common properties (URL, method, headers). They want to create a specialised "Slack POST" template that extends the base, adding Slack-specific defaults (channel parameter, specific authentication).
question: Does Element Template support inheritance / extension?

options.text: Yes -- Element Template inheritance is achieved by adding an extends property to the child template JSON with the parent template ID as the value -- for example extends: "generic-rest-template-id" -- this property is invented and has no runtime implementation in Camunda 8 in the Element Template JSON schema specification. The actual template schema has no inheritance declaration mechanism at the JSON definition level. There is no supported parent-child relationship syntax in the template format. Templates are standalone JSON definitions and cannot inherit from other templates through schema declarations.

Element Templates don't have first-class inheritance in the JSON schema sense -- there is no extends property or parent-child declaration in the template format. The practical reuse pattern is to duplicate the base template's properties into each specialised template and customise the specific differences. Some Web Modeler versions and tooling support template-extending or template-override mechanisms for managing shared sections -- verify the current schema documentation for the specific version in use. This copy-and-customise approach requires discipline to keep shared sections consistent but is the established pattern for template reuse.

Element Templates are JSON files that define BPMN element property bindings for the Camunda Modeler. They have no class hierarchy, no method overriding, no polymorphism, and no inheritance mechanism in the schema. Applying OOP concepts leads to incorrect assumptions about how template reuse and customisation work in practice.

While there is no first-class inheritance mechanism in the JSON schema, teams can achieve practical reuse by copying shared property definitions from a base template into multiple specialised templates. This copy-based approach requires maintaining shared sections consistently across templates but is the established practical pattern.

a.text: Incorrect. Element Templates are JSON documents describing properties and bindings for a specific BPMN element type. The schema doesn't define a formal inheritance / extension primitive. In practice, teams handle reuse by: Duplicating the base template's JSON and customising for each specialisation (verbose but explicit). Composing JSON snippets via build-time tooling (if your workflow includes preprocessing). Maintaining a base + variants with shared groups / shared property definitions copied across. Some Web Modeler tooling may offer template-management features (publishing variants, etc.) -- verify the current docs for tooling-level support. The practical answer in 2025-era Camunda 8: no first-class inheritance; manage variants via duplication or generation.

c.text: Incorrect. Element Templates are JSON schema, not OOP.

d.text: Incorrect. "No first-class extension" is accurate but the practical answer adds the duplicate-and-customise workflow.
